roadmap.title: Backend Java Spring Boot Developer
roadmap.description: A realistic beginner-to-junior backend roadmap focused on Java, Spring Boot, relational data, API design, testing, and deployment habits that matter in day-to-day product work.

nodes.title: Programming Fundamentals
nodes.summary: Build the habits that make every later backend topic easier: control flow, functions, debugging, and problem decomposition.
nodes.note: Treat this node as a thinking-skills foundation, not just a syntax checkpoint.
checklist: Write pseudocode for a console program that validates input and repeats until the user exits.
Trace the program by hand for at least three inputs and record each variable value.
Implement and debug three beginner problems, documenting how each problem was decomposed.

resources.title: CS50 Introduction to Computer Science
resources.contentBody: Use the early lectures to strengthen computational thinking and debugging discipline.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Introduction to Algorithms
resources.contentBody: Practice representing, comparing, and reasoning about algorithms before tying the ideas to Java syntax.

nodes.title: Java Basics
nodes.summary: Learn Java syntax, types, methods, and the standard toolchain so you can read and write simple console programs comfortably.
nodes.note: Focus on writing many tiny examples instead of reading passively.
checklist: Create, compile, and run a small Java console application from both an IDE and the command line.
Build a grade calculator that uses primitive types, strings, arrays, control flow, and methods.
Record and explain fixes for five compiler or runtime errors encountered while building the exercises.

resources.title: The Java Tutorials: Language Basics
resources.contentBody: Covers variables, operators, statements, and other syntax essentials.

resources.title: Dev.java Learn Java
resources.contentBody: Modern Java learning material straight from the Java platform team.

nodes.title: OOP in Java
nodes.summary: Move from isolated syntax to real object modeling with classes, objects, encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.
nodes.note: The goal is not to memorize jargon; it is to model simple domains with clear responsibilities.
checklist: Model a library domain with classes, private fields, constructors, and behavior-focused methods.
Implement one relationship with composition and explain why inheritance would be weaker there.
Refactor a procedural console program into objects with clear, non-overlapping responsibilities.

resources.title: The Java Tutorials: Object-Oriented Programming Concepts
resources.contentBody: A clean introduction to the object model used throughout Java codebases.

resources.title: Refactoring Guru: Design Patterns in Java
resources.contentBody: Skim the early examples to see how object design affects maintainability.

nodes.title: Collections and Generics
nodes.summary: Understand lists, sets, maps, iterators, and generics so you can model collections safely and idiomatically.
nodes.note: This node pays off everywhere in backend code because data almost always flows through collections.
checklist: Implement the same lookup requirement with List, Set, and Map and compare their semantics.
Create a reusable generic container or utility without raw types or unchecked casts.
Transform, filter, group, and sort a collection of domain objects with tests for duplicate values.

resources.title: The Java Tutorials: Collections
resources.contentBody: Explains the core collection interfaces and common usage patterns.

resources.title: The Java Tutorials: Generics
resources.contentBody: Shows how Java generics protect you from type bugs and noisy casts.

challenge.title: Build a Type-Safe Library Catalog
challenge.description: Implement a small Java library catalog that uses collections and generics to store books, reject duplicate identifiers, and answer common lookup and grouping questions.
challenge.instructions: Fork or copy the starter repository, implement the catalog classes in the provided package, keep the public method signatures unchanged, and submit the exact full commit SHA that contains your solution.
acceptanceCriteria: The catalog stores books without raw collection types or unchecked casts.
Adding the same ISBN twice does not create a duplicate entry.
Books can be found by ISBN and grouped by author with deterministic results.
The supplied Maven test suite passes without modifying grader-owned tests.
hints: Choose collection implementations based on identity, ordering, and lookup requirements.
Use bounded generic methods only where the algorithm needs a specific capability.

nodes.title: SQL Basics
nodes.summary: Learn how relational data is stored and queried so your backend code can ask useful questions of a database.
nodes.note: Stay grounded in tables, joins, and constraints before jumping into ORMs.
checklist: Create a small PostgreSQL schema with primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, and sample rows.
Write and execute SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements against the schema.
Answer five domain questions with INNER and LEFT joins, then verify the returned row shapes.

resources.title: PostgreSQL Tutorial: SQL
resources.contentBody: A practical reference for real SQL syntax and relational concepts.

resources.title: SQLBolt
resources.contentBody: Use the interactive lessons to make simple SQL muscle memory stick.

nodes.title: JDBC Basics
nodes.summary: Connect Java code to a database directly so you understand what frameworks later automate for you.
nodes.note: Do not aim for perfect architecture here; aim to understand the plumbing and pain points.
checklist: Connect a Java console application to PostgreSQL with a JDBC URL and close resources safely.
Implement create and find operations with PreparedStatement and parameter binding.
Map a multi-row ResultSet into typed Java objects and test the empty-result case.

resources.title: The Java Tutorials: JDBC Basics
resources.contentBody: Explains the database access flow that Spring Data eventually abstracts away.

resources.title: Baeldung JDBC Guide
resources.contentBody: A quick practical walkthrough for simple JDBC setup and usage.

challenge.title: Build a JDBC Book Repository
challenge.description: Implement a JDBC repository for books using prepared statements, explicit row mapping, and safe resource handling.
challenge.instructions: Complete the repository methods in the starter project, keep SQL parameterized, preserve the supplied schema contract, and submit the full commit SHA for the finished implementation.
acceptanceCriteria: Create and lookup operations use PreparedStatement parameters instead of SQL string concatenation.
Every Connection, PreparedStatement, and ResultSet is closed safely.
Rows are mapped into typed Book objects and an absent row is represented explicitly.
Repository tests pass against the grader-managed database fixture.
hints: Use try-with-resources for every JDBC resource acquired by a method.
Keep row mapping in one focused method so all queries use the same mapping rules.

nodes.title: Spring Core
nodes.summary: Understand dependency injection, beans, and application context fundamentals before relying on Spring Boot magic.
nodes.note: If you understand bean wiring and inversion of control, debugging Boot apps gets much easier.
checklist: Build a small Spring context with service and repository beans wired through constructor injection.
Replace one component-scanned bean with an explicit @Bean definition and explain the trade-off.
Use two profiles or qualifiers to select implementations and verify which bean the context loads.

resources.title: Spring Framework Reference: Core Technologies
resources.contentBody: The best long-term reference for the container and bean model.

resources.title: Spring Framework Reference: The IoC Container
resources.contentBody: Study bean definitions, dependency injection, component scanning, scopes, and Java-based container configuration.

nodes.title: Spring Boot Basics
nodes.summary: Learn the conventions, auto-configuration, starter dependencies, and application structure used in modern Spring Boot services.
nodes.note: Get comfortable reading configuration and understanding what Boot has already set up for you.
checklist: Generate a Spring Boot project with Spring Initializr and run it through Maven and the IDE.
Add typed application configuration and override it with a local profile and environment variable.
Inspect the condition evaluation report and explain two auto-configurations activated by the starters.

resources.title: Spring Boot Documentation
resources.contentBody: Use this as the main reference for Boot setup, configuration, and testing later on.

resources.title: Spring Guide: Building an Application with Spring Boot
resources.contentBody: A small, low-friction guide for seeing the framework pieces work together.

nodes.title: REST API Development
nodes.summary: Build HTTP APIs with Spring MVC, request validation, clear responses, and resource-oriented endpoint design.
nodes.note: This is where the roadmap starts to feel like product development instead of framework study.
checklist: Implement CRUD endpoints with thin controllers, request DTOs, response DTOs, and a service layer.
Add Bean Validation and a global error handler that returns consistent 400 and 404 responses.
Exercise the API with curl or an API client and document status codes for success and failure cases.

resources.title: Spring Guide: Building a RESTful Web Service
resources.contentBody: A solid first pass at request handling, controllers, and response shaping.

resources.title: REST API Tutorial
resources.contentBody: Use this to sharpen naming, status-code, and payload design decisions.

challenge.title: Build a Book Catalog REST API
challenge.description: Create a Spring Boot API for books and authors with pagination, validation, exception handling, and consistent JSON responses. Include endpoints for listing, creating, updating, and deleting books, and document at least one tradeoff you made in the design.
challenge.instructions: Extend the starter catalog into a Spring Boot REST API. Preserve the documented endpoint paths, return DTOs instead of entities, validate incoming data, and submit the exact full commit SHA for evaluation.
acceptanceCriteria: Book create, list, update, and delete endpoints follow the supplied HTTP contract.
Invalid payloads and missing records return consistent error responses with appropriate status codes.
List responses support the required pagination parameters.
Controller responses do not expose persistence entities directly.
hints: Keep request handling in controllers and application rules in services.
Use one exception handler to keep error payloads consistent.

nodes.title: Spring Data JPA
nodes.summary: Map Java objects to relational tables, define repositories, and reason about persistence without losing sight of SQL behavior.
nodes.note: JPA becomes much less mysterious if you keep checking the SQL shape underneath.
checklist: Map two related entities with database constraints and keep entities out of controller responses.
Implement repository queries for lookup, pagination, and one relationship-aware use case.
Capture generated SQL for a lazy-loading scenario and remove the resulting N+1 query.

resources.title: Spring Guide: Accessing Data with JPA
resources.contentBody: A compact starting point for entities, repositories, and persistence wiring.

resources.title: Spring Data JPA Reference
resources.contentBody: Use this reference when you need repository patterns beyond the basics.

challenge.title: Persist the Book Catalog with JPA
challenge.description: Extend the REST API by storing books, authors, and categories in PostgreSQL using JPA entities and repository methods. Add at least one relationship, one uniqueness rule, and one query method that would matter in a real product.
challenge.instructions: Persist the cumulative Book Catalog with Spring Data JPA. Implement the supplied entity relationships and repository behavior without changing the public REST contract, then submit the full commit SHA.
acceptanceCriteria: Book and Author entities enforce the supplied database constraints and relationship ownership.
Repository queries support pagination and the required author lookup.
The implementation avoids the tested N+1 query path.
API responses remain DTO-based and persistence details do not leak into JSON.
hints: Model database constraints explicitly instead of relying only on service validation.
Inspect generated SQL when choosing fetch joins or entity graphs.

nodes.title: Spring Security and JWT
nodes.summary: Protect APIs with authentication and authorization while understanding the request filter chain and token lifecycle.
nodes.note: Security work gets much easier when you can explain every step from login to protected endpoint access.
checklist: Diagram the request path from credential verification through JWT issuance and filter-chain authentication.
Protect public, authenticated, and role-restricted endpoints and test each access outcome.
Hash passwords, validate token expiry, and confirm responses and logs never expose secrets or password hashes.

resources.title: Spring Security Reference
resources.contentBody: Keep this open while learning the filter chain and endpoint protection.

resources.title: JWT Introduction
resources.contentBody: A concise primer on what JWTs are good at and what they are not.

challenge.title: Add Auth to the Catalog API
challenge.description: Implement signup and login for the catalog API, issue JWTs, protect write endpoints, and enforce at least one role-based rule. Document how an unauthenticated request and an authenticated request should differ.
challenge.instructions: Add JWT authentication to the cumulative catalog project using the supplied endpoint and role contract. Never commit secrets, keep password hashes out of responses, and submit the exact full commit SHA.
acceptanceCriteria: Signup hashes passwords and never returns stored password data.
Login returns a valid signed JWT for correct credentials and rejects incorrect credentials.
Public, authenticated, and role-restricted routes enforce the supplied access rules.
Expired or malformed tokens fail safely without exposing internal details.
hints: Keep JWT parsing in the security boundary instead of controllers.
Test authorization separately from authentication so failures are easier to diagnose.

nodes.title: Testing Basics
nodes.summary: Write tests that protect behavior: repository tests for persistence rules, service tests for logic, and controller tests for contracts.
nodes.note: Aim for confidence around behavior, not coverage numbers for their own sake.
checklist: Write unit tests for service behavior with clear arrange-act-assert structure and descriptive names.
Add a repository or MVC slice test for one persistence or HTTP contract boundary.
Cover validation, not-found, and authorization failures in addition to the happy path.

resources.title: JUnit 5 User Guide
resources.contentBody: A durable reference for modern JUnit structure and assertions.

resources.title: Spring Boot Testing Reference
resources.contentBody: Covers the test slices and integration patterns most useful in Spring projects.

challenge.title: Add Regression Tests for the Catalog API
challenge.description: Create a test suite that covers at least one repository constraint, one service-level business rule, one validation failure, and one secured endpoint behavior. The suite should be fast enough to run regularly during development.
challenge.instructions: Add focused JUnit tests to the cumulative catalog project. Keep tests deterministic and isolated, use the narrowest suitable Spring test slice, and submit the exact full commit SHA.
acceptanceCriteria: A repository test proves one database constraint or query contract.
A service test covers one success path and one business-rule failure.
An MVC test covers validation and a secured endpoint response.
The complete Maven test suite passes repeatedly without ordering dependencies.
hints: Prefer behavior-focused assertions over implementation details.
Use explicit test fixtures so each test communicates why its data matters.

nodes.title: Docker Basics
nodes.summary: Package the service and its dependencies so local setup and handoff become more predictable.
nodes.note: You do not need deep container theory yet; focus on running your app consistently.
checklist: Write a Dockerfile, build the backend image, and run it with explicit environment configuration.
Use Docker Compose to connect the app, PostgreSQL, and Redis with persistent database storage.
Inspect images, containers, networks, and volumes, then explain which artifact stores each kind of state.

resources.title: Docker Get Started
resources.contentBody: A practical introduction to images, containers, and local workflows.

resources.title: Docker Compose Overview
resources.contentBody: Use this to understand service orchestration for app plus database setups.

challenge.title: Containerize the Book Catalog
challenge.description: Package the cumulative catalog API in a reproducible container and run it with PostgreSQL through Docker Compose.
challenge.instructions: Add a production-oriented Dockerfile and Compose definition for the catalog. Keep secrets external, preserve the supplied service contract, and submit the exact full commit SHA.
acceptanceCriteria: The application image builds from the submitted repository using the supplied command.
The application container runs as a non-root user and exposes the expected health endpoint.
Compose connects the application and PostgreSQL without hard-coded credentials.
Build context and final image exclude source-control metadata and local secrets.
hints: Use a multi-stage build to keep build tools out of the runtime image.
Use environment interpolation and document required variables in an example file.

nodes.title: Deployment Basics
nodes.summary: Learn the final mile of shipping: environment configuration, build artifacts, containers, and a simple deployment workflow.
nodes.note: The goal is a boring, repeatable release flow you can explain clearly.
checklist: Externalize database credentials, JWT configuration, and environment-specific settings without committing secrets.
Create a repeatable workflow that tests, builds, and publishes the deployable container artifact.
Run a deployment rehearsal using health checks, structured logs, a schema-change check, and a documented rollback.

resources.title: Spring Boot Externalized Configuration
resources.contentBody: Explains how to move environment-specific values and secrets out of the application artifact for deployment.

resources.title: GitHub Actions Deployments
resources.contentBody: A lightweight reference for automating build and deployment steps.

challenge.title: Ship the Catalog API
challenge.description: Containerize the catalog API, configure environment variables cleanly, and prepare a basic deployment workflow for a staging environment. Include a short release checklist covering migration safety, health verification, and rollback steps.
challenge.instructions: Finish the cumulative catalog project with a deterministic CI workflow and an operational release plan. The grader validates configuration and workflow behavior without requiring production credentials. Submit the exact full commit SHA.
acceptanceCriteria: The CI workflow runs tests and builds the deployable image from a clean checkout.
Runtime configuration is externalized and the repository contains no real credentials.
A health check and release checklist cover migration, verification, and rollback.
The documented deployment procedure identifies immutable artifact versions.
hints: Treat the commit SHA or image digest as the release identity.
Make rollback a concrete command sequence instead of a general intention.
